claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have a child I'm responsible for. I have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know that you have Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with you.

To continue assessing your eligibility, could you tell me where you currently live? Specifically, do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you! Living in the UK and having Alex (8 years old) living with you are important factors for eligibility.

Now I need to ask: Is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or in any residential accommodation? Or does Alex live with you full-time at home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in hospital for a while now - about 3 or 4 months. But I still regularly spend money on things for them, like their favourite snacks, books, and clothes while they're in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital for such an extended period.

The fact that you're regularly spending money on Alex while they're in hospital is very relevant to your eligibility. Just to clarify: Has Alex been in hospital continuously for this entire 3-4 month period, or have there been any breaks where Alex came home for a day or more?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's been continuous - Alex has been in the hospital ward the whole time without coming home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have enough information now to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:

- Alex is 8 years old (under 16) ✓
- Alex normally lives with you in the UK ✓
- Alex has been in hospital continuously for 3-4 months (which exceeds the 12-week limit)
- However, you regularly spend money on Alex while they're in hospital

According to the Child Benefit rules, there is an important exception: even after the 12-week limit for children in UK hospitals, you can still remain entitled to Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on them - which you are doing by buying snacks, books, and clothes for Alex.

This means you should still be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Even though Alex has been in hospital for longer than the standard 12-week period, you qualify under an important exception: because you regularly spend money on Alex while they're in hospital (buying snacks, books, and clothes), you can continue to receive Child Benefit.

If you haven't already claimed Child Benefit, you can make a claim. If you're already receiving it, you should continue to be entitled to it as long as you keep regularly spending money on Alex during their hospital stay.

I hope Alex recovers soon and is able to come home!

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), normally lives with the claimant in the UK, and the claimant is responsible for Alex. Although Alex has been in a UK hospital continuously for 3-4 months (exceeding the 12-week limit), there is an exception to the rule that applies: for children in UK hospitals, the claimant can remain entitled to Child Benefit after 12 weeks if they regularly spend money on the child. The claimant confirmed they regularly spend money on Alex for snacks, books, and clothes while in hospital, so this exception applies and they remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 8-year-old child Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital continuously for 3-4 months (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), the claimant regularly spends money on Alex during the hospital stay. This meets the exception criteria that allows Child Benefit to continue for children in UK hospitals beyond 12 weeks when the parent regularly spends money on them. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Although Alex has been in a UK hospital continuously for 3-4 months (exceeding the 12-week limit), there is an exception to the rule that applies: for children in UK hospitals, the claimant can remain entitled to Child Benefit after 12 weeks if they regularly spend money on the child. The claimant confirmed they regularly spend money on Alex for snacks, books, and clothes while in hospital, so this exception applies and they remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 8-year-old child Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital continuously for 3-4 months (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), the claimant regularly spends money on Alex during the hospital stay. This meets the exception criteria that allows Child Benefit to continue for children in UK hospitals beyond 12 weeks when the parent regularly spends money on them. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
